definitions.definition: Sports nutrition refers to the study and practice of nutrition and diet with regards to improving anyone's athletic performance. It is a field of sports medicine that focuses on the type and amount of food that an athlete should consume in order to perform at maximum efficiency.
definitions.example: A football player may require a diet rich in carbohydrates to improve endurance.

definitions.definition: Macronutrients are nutrients that provide energy and support growth and maintenance of body tissues. The three main macronutrients are carbohydrates, proteins, and fats.
definitions.example: A basketball player may require a diet high in carbohydrates to provide energy for performance.

definitions.definition: Carbohydrates are the body's primary source of energy. They come in two forms: simple carbohydrates (sugars) and complex carbohydrates (starches and fibers).
definitions.example: Eating a meal rich in complex carbohydrates such as brown rice, whole wheat bread, and fruits before a game can help provide sustained energy.

definitions.definition: Protein is a macronutrient that helps build and repair muscles, bones, and other body tissues. It is essential for muscle growth and recovery after exercise.
definitions.example: A wrestler may require a diet high in protein to help build and repair muscle tissue.

definitions.definition: Fats are a type of macronutrient that provide energy and help absorb certain vitamins. They come in two forms: saturated and unsaturated fats.
definitions.example: Nuts and seeds are a good source of healthy unsaturated fats that can help provide energy for athletes.

definitions.definition: Micronutrients are vitamins and minerals that are essential for maintaining good health and preventing diseases. They do not provide energy but help regulate various bodily functions.
definitions.example: Vitamin C is a micronutrient that helps boost the immune system and reduce the risk of illness in athletes.

definitions.definition: Vitamins are micronutrients that are essential for maintaining good health. They help regulate various bodily functions such as energy production, immune function, and bone health.
definitions.example: Vitamin D is essential for maintaining strong bones and preventing injuries in athletes.

definitions.definition: Minerals are micronutrients that are essential for maintaining good health. They help regulate various bodily functions such as muscle function, nerve function, and hydration.
definitions.example: Iron is a mineral that helps transport oxygen to the muscles and prevent fatigue in athletes.

definitions.definition: Hydration refers to the process of providing the body with enough water to function properly. Adequate hydration is essential for athletic performance and overall health.
definitions.example: Drinking water before, during, and after exercise can help prevent dehydration and maintain athletic performance.

definitions.definition: Dehydration is a condition that occurs when the body loses more fluids than it takes in. It can lead to decreased athletic performance, fatigue, and other health problems.
definitions.example: An athlete who does not drink enough water during a game may become dehydrated and experience decreased performance.

definitions.definition: Electrolytes are minerals that carry an electric charge and help regulate various bodily functions such as hydration, muscle function, and nerve function.
definitions.example: Sodium and potassium are electrolytes that help regulate hydration and muscle function in athletes.

definitions.definition: Glycogen is a complex carbohydrate that is stored in the liver and muscles. It serves as a readily available source of energy for the body.
definitions.example: Eating a meal rich in carbohydrates before a game can help replenish glycogen stores and provide energy for performance.

definitions.definition: Ketosis is a metabolic state in which the body burns fat for fuel instead of carbohydrates. It can occur when an athlete is on a low-carbohydrate diet or fasting.
definitions.example: A low-carbohydrate diet may induce ketosis, which can help an athlete lose weight but may also decrease athletic performance.

definitions.definition: Nutrient timing refers to the practice of consuming specific nutrients at specific times to optimize athletic performance and recovery.
definitions.example: Consuming a meal rich in carbohydrates and protein within 30-60 minutes after exercise can help replenish energy stores and support muscle recovery.

definitions.definition: Meal frequency refers to the number of meals an athlete consumes per day. Eating frequent, balanced meals can help support athletic performance and recovery.
definitions.example: Eating 5-6 meals per day, including 3 main meals and 2-3 snacks, can help provide a steady supply of energy and nutrients for athletes.

definitions.definition: Supplements are products that contain one or more nutrients, such as vitamins, minerals, or amino acids, that are intended to supplement the diet and support athletic performance.
definitions.example: Protein powder is a supplement that can help athletes increase their protein intake and support muscle growth and recovery.

definitions.definition: Ergogenic aids are substances or devices that are intended to enhance athletic performance. They can include supplements, equipment, and other products.
definitions.example: Creatine is an ergogenic aid that can help increase muscle strength and endurance in athletes.
